articles.title: Scale the S-CSCF worker pool when server-side latency and 5xx errors rise together
articles.summary: Server internal errors often start with worker saturation on the serving call-control path.
content: When to use: Server-side latency, queueing, and 5xx responses all rise on the S-CSCF or equivalent control-plane tier.
Action: Add capacity to the overloaded worker pool, then verify requests are redistributed across the new replicas.
Why it works: Scaling the saturated tier reduces queueing pressure and shortens the window for cascading internal errors.
Verify: Error rate, queue depth, and per-pod CPU normalize within the affected service tier.
Follow-up: Capture the scaling threshold that stabilized the service so it can guide future remediation ranking.

articles.title: Clear a stuck HSS or database connection pool causing internal failures
articles.summary: A degraded dependency pool can generate server errors even when application code is unchanged.
content: When to use: Internal errors spike after backend latency grows and one service shows many stalled dependency connections.
Action: Inspect the connection pool, drain bad connections, and confirm the backend is accepting new healthy sessions.
Why it works: Connection pool exhaustion traps the app in timeouts or failed retries that present as generic 5xx errors.
Verify: New backend calls complete normally and server error volume drops without another app rollout.
Risk: Reconnecting too aggressively can overwhelm a recovering backend, so stagger if necessary.

articles.title: Rollback a failing config push that widened the server error blast radius
articles.summary: If internal errors begin immediately after deployment, a targeted rollback is often the safest first action.
content: When to use: Error volume jumps right after a service config rollout, feature flag change, or policy push.
Action: Restore the last known-good revision, preserve the failing diff, and confirm the service recovers on the previous state.
Why it works: Rolling back narrows the troubleshooting surface and quickly stabilizes the control plane during a live incident.
Verify: The prior revision returns the service to normal latency and error levels.
Aftercare: Reproduce the issue offline with the captured diff before attempting another rollout.

articles.title: Inspect memory pressure and garbage-collection churn on overloaded server pods
articles.summary: Memory pressure can trigger latency spikes, crash loops, and internal error bursts.
content: When to use: Pods remain running but latency rises sharply and restarts or GC pauses correlate with internal errors.
Action: Compare heap growth, memory limits, and garbage-collection behavior against a healthy baseline for the same service.
Why it works: Internal failures may be a symptom of resource starvation rather than a functional defect.
Verify: Memory behavior stabilizes and error spikes stop following GC or OOM events.
Guardrail: Raising limits alone can hide leaks, so pair capacity relief with leak investigation.

articles.title: Drain a crashing pod from service before it poisons more traffic
articles.summary: One unstable pod can create a disproportionate share of internal errors.
content: When to use: Failures cluster on one pod or node while peers on the same revision remain healthy.
Action: Remove the unstable instance from the load-balancer path, replace it cleanly, and observe whether the incident follows the instance.
Why it works: Isolating a bad replica prevents localized failure from becoming a full fleet problem.
Verify: Errors stop hitting the drained instance and traffic succeeds on healthy peers.
Risk: Do not drain the pod unless the remaining pool has room to absorb the traffic.

articles.title: Verify database latency guardrails before tuning the application
articles.summary: A slow persistence path can surface as generic server errors in upstream SIP services.
content: When to use: The service emits internal errors while waiting on data reads or writes to complete.
Action: Compare backend database latency, timeout settings, and retry behavior with the application's current guardrails.
Why it works: Many server failures are really timeout-policy mismatches against a slowing data tier.
Verify: Database operations complete inside the configured budget and upstream 5xx rates fall.
Escalate if: Database latency is broadly elevated across unrelated services, which points to a larger platform problem.

articles.title: Raise or restore file-descriptor limits when connection churn is high
articles.summary: Connection-heavy SIP workloads can hit OS limits and create server-side failures.
content: When to use: Logs show socket or file-handle exhaustion during traffic surges or after scaling events.
Action: Confirm current descriptor limits, restore expected values, and check whether connection churn patterns recently changed.
Why it works: Server internal errors at the OS boundary often look like random app failures until resource ceilings are inspected.
Verify: New connections succeed under load and the service stops logging descriptor-related failures.
Guardrail: Address churn sources as well, otherwise the higher limit only delays recurrence.

articles.title: Isolate a noisy-neighbor or degraded node affecting one service tier
articles.summary: Node-level contention can create server errors without any application code change.
content: When to use: One node hosts pods with unusual CPU steal, disk wait, or network contention that matches the incident timeline.
Action: Move the affected workload to healthier nodes or drain the degraded node if the cluster has headroom.
Why it works: Removing hidden infrastructure contention often resolves unexplained latency and error bursts quickly.
Verify: Error rates follow the workload off the bad node and normalize on healthy infrastructure.
Evidence: Preserve node-level metrics so the issue is not misclassified as an application regression.

articles.title: Enable or tighten circuit breaking on a failing dependency path
articles.summary: A hard-failing dependency can cascade internal errors unless the app sheds that load quickly.
content: When to use: One downstream dependency is clearly degraded and repeated retries are overwhelming the calling service.
Action: Tighten circuit-breaker or retry-budget settings so the service fails fast instead of saturating itself.
Why it works: Controlled failure protects the rest of the call-control path while the dependency is repaired.
Verify: Upstream latency and error amplification drop once the service stops retrying into the failing backend.
Risk: Failing fast changes the user-visible behavior, so document the trade-off in the incident record.

articles.title: Restore the cache warm path after a cold restart
articles.summary: Cold caches after restart can mimic a persistent server fault until the warm path is restored.
content: When to use: Error rates spike after a restart even though the new pods are healthy and dependencies are available.
Action: Check whether caches, lookup tables, or service discovery data are repopulating too slowly for the live traffic rate.
Why it works: Warm-path gaps create temporary overload and timeout behavior that looks like a true regression.
Verify: Cache hit rate improves and the service exits the high-error state without further code changes.
Aftercare: Add a warm-up or readiness gate so new instances do not receive full traffic before they are prepared.
